source_paper_title: Exactness and Effective Degree Bound of Lasserre’s Relaxation for Polynomial Optimization over Finite Variety
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Hua_2025, title={Exactness and Effective Degree Bound of Lasserre’s Relaxation for Polynomial Optimization over Finite Variety}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0483}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0483}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Hua, Zheng and Qu, Zheng}, year={2025}, month=july }
context:
Section 8 (Conclusion and perspectives), page 21 (in the provided text, the paragraph beginning 'However, Assumption 1 is stronger ...')

However, Assumption 1 is stronger than the regular sequence property of \(\{\bar g_1, \ldots, \bar g_n\}\), see Section C. Indeed, \(\{\bar g_1, \ldots, \bar g_n\}\) is still a regular sequence if \(\{g^\infty_1, \ldots, g^\infty_n\}\) has finitely many complex common zero locus. However, the H-basis property may fail if we only assume that \(\{\bar g_1, \ldots, \bar g_n\}\) is a regular sequence. It would be interesting to investigate the degree bound under the regular sequence condition only.
title: Degree bound for Lasserre relaxation under regular sequence assumptions without infinity condition
background_latex:
Consider a polynomial optimization problem in \(n\) real variables
\[
\min_{x\in\mathbb R^n} f(x)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad g_1(x)=\cdots=g_k(x)=0,\; h_1(x)\ge 0,\dots,h_m(x)\ge 0,
\]
where \(f,g_1,\dots,g_k,h_1,\dots,h_m\in\mathbb R[x_1,\dots,x_n]\). Assume the complex variety of equality constraints is finite:
\[
V_{\mathbb C}(g_1,\dots,g_k):=\{z\in\mathbb C^n: g_1(z)=\cdots=g_k(z)=0\}
\]
is nonempty and consists of finitely many points, and the feasible set is nonempty with optimal value \(f^*\).

For an integer \(d\ge 0\), define the truncated ideal and truncated quadratic module
\[
I(g_1,\dots,g_k)_d:=\Big\{\sum_{i=1}^k \lambda_i g_i : \lambda_i\in\mathbb R[x],\;\deg(\lambda_i g_i)\le d\Big\},
\]
\[
QM(1,h_1,\dots,h_m)_d:=\Big\{\sum_{j=0}^m \sigma_j h_j : \sigma_j\in\Sigma[x],\;\deg(\sigma_j h_j)\le d\Big\},\quad h_0\equiv 1,
\]
where \(\Sigma[x]\) is the cone of sums-of-squares polynomials.

Lasserre’s SOS relaxation of order \(d\) is exact (and attains) if
\[
 f-f^*\in I(g_1,\dots,g_k)_{2d}+QM(1,h_1,\dots,h_m)_{2d}.
\]

For each \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\), let \(g_i^\infty\) denote the highest-degree homogeneous part of \(g_i\) (equivalently, the dehomogenization at \(x_0=0\) of the homogenization of \(g_i\)). The paper imposes a strong geometric condition (no solutions at infinity) that implies that the homogenizations \(\bar g_1,\dots,\bar g_n\in\mathbb R[x_0,\dots,x_n]\) form a regular sequence and, crucially, that \(g_1,\dots,g_n\) form an \(H\)-basis of \(\langle g_1,\dots,g_n\rangle\), enabling an explicit degree bound.

A weaker algebraic condition mentioned in the paper is that \(\bar g_1,\dots,\bar g_n\) form a regular sequence in \(\mathbb R[x_0,\dots,x_n]\). This can hold even when \(g_1^\infty,\dots,g_n^\infty\) have finitely many common complex zeros (i.e., solutions at infinity exist), and in that case the \(H\)-basis property may fail.
problem_definition_latex:
Assume \(k\ge n\) and \(V_{\mathbb C}(g_1,\dots,g_k)\) is finite. Suppose the homogenizations \(\bar g_1,\dots,\bar g_n\in\mathbb R[x_0,\dots,x_n]\) form a regular sequence (but do not assume the stronger condition that \(g_1^\infty(z)=\cdots=g_n^\infty(z)=0\) has no solution in \(\mathbb C^n\setminus\{0\}\)).

Determine an explicit bound \(d\) (as a function of the degrees of \(f,g_1,\dots,g_k,h_1,\dots,h_m\), and possibly other algebraic invariants naturally implied by the regular-sequence hypothesis) such that exactness at order \(d\) holds, i.e.
\[
 f-f^*\in I(g_1,\dots,g_k)_{2d}+QM(1,h_1,\dots,h_m)_{2d},
\]
under the same type of nonsingularity/rank assumptions at minimizers used in the paper to ensure membership at some finite order.

Equivalently, establish effective degree bounds for exactness of Lasserre’s hierarchy when only the regular-sequence condition on \(\bar g_1,\dots,\bar g_n\) is assumed, despite possible failure of the \(H\)-basis property.

Suppose the homogenizations \(\bar g_1,\dots,\bar g_n\in\mathbb R[x_0,\dots,x_n]\) form a regular sequence (but do not assume the stronger condition that \(g_1^\infty(z)=\cdots=g_n^\infty(z)=0\) has no solution in \(\mathbb C^n\setminus\{0\}\)).

\]

location_in_paper: Section 8 (Conclusion and perspectives), page 21 (in the provided text, the paragraph beginning 'However, Assumption 1 is stronger ...')
open_problem_quote: However, Assumption 1 is stronger than the regular sequence property of \(\{\bar g_1, \ldots, \bar g_n\}\), see Section C. Indeed, \(\{\bar g_1, \ldots, \bar g_n\}\) is still a regular sequence if \(\{g^\infty_1, \ldots, g^\infty_n\}\) has finitely many complex common zero locus. However, the H-basis property may fail if we only assume that \(\{\bar g_1, \ldots, \bar g_n\}\) is a regular sequence. It would be interesting to investigate the degree bound under the regular sequence condition only.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally generate small-degree polynomial systems where the homogenizations form a regular sequence but the H-basis property fails, and then test empirically how large a relaxation order is needed for exactness. However, verifying the regular-sequence property and exactness degree can require Gröbner basis computations and SDP solves, which limits large-scale brute-force search.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate instances (polynomial systems) and candidate bounds can be represented programmatically, with a fitness signal based on computed minimal relaxation order for exactness (via SDP/Gröbner computations) versus proposed formulas. The expensive algebraic checks and SDP scaling make the feedback loop feasible only for small sizes/degrees, but it could still uncover patterns or sharpness examples.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Hua–Qu (2025) obtain an explicit order bound for exactness of Lasserre’s hierarchy in the zero-dimensional (finite complex variety) setting by imposing a geometric “no solutions at infinity” condition on \(g_1^\infty,\dots,g_n^\infty\). This condition implies that the homogenizations \(\bar g_1,\dots,\bar g_n\) form a regular sequence and, crucially, that \(g_1,\dots,g_n\) form an \(H\)-basis, letting one control truncation degrees in ideal representations and hence bound the relaxation order \(d\) purely in terms of input degrees.

The open problem asks for comparable explicit bounds when one assumes only that \(\bar g_1,\dots,\bar g_n\) is a regular sequence, allowing solutions at infinity and potential failure of the \(H\)-basis property. The only forward-citing work provided, “An effective positivstellensatz over the rational numbers for finite semialgebraic sets,” gives explicit degree bounds for SOS/Positivstellensatz certificates on finite semialgebraic sets, but its bounds require that the equality generators form a graded basis (a degree-compatibility condition closely aligned with having an \(H\)-basis). It therefore does not yet bridge the gap from regular-sequence-only hypotheses to effective truncation bounds.

At present, the main gap is to replace the \(H\)-basis/graded-basis input by invariants implied by regular sequences with possible roots at infinity, e.g., Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity of the homogenized ideal, degrees of a border basis, or bounds derived from syzygies of a complete intersection with embedded components at infinity. Promising directions include deriving degree-controlled normal form reductions modulo \(\langle g_1,\dots,g_n\rangle\) using regularity and saturation with respect to \(x_0\), and translating those into explicit truncation bounds for \(I(g)_{2d}\) in the SOS certificate for \(f-f^*\).

sources.title: An effective positivstellensatz over the rational numbers for finite semialgebraic sets
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves an effective Putinar/Parrilo-type Positivstellensatz for finite semialgebraic sets with explicit degree bounds in terms of a monomial basis degree (regularity) when the equality ideal has a graded basis. Concretely (Theorem A / Theorems 2.7 & 3.2): for a finite set 
\[S=\{x\in\mathbb R^n: g_i(x)\ge 0,\ h_j(x)=0\},\quad I=(h)\ \text{zero-dimensional},\]
if either (i) \(f>0\) on \(S\), or (ii) \(f\ge 0\) on \(S\) and \((I:f)+(f)=(1)\), then
\[f=\sum_{k=1}^D \omega_{0,k}q_{0,k}^2+\sum_{i=1}^r\Big(\sum_{k=1}^D\omega_{i,k}q_{i,k}^2\Big)g_i+\sum_{j=1}^s p_j h_j,\]
with \(\omega_{i,k}\in K_{\ge0}\), \(q_{i,k}\in\langle B\rangle_K\) (\(B\) a monomial basis of \(K[x]/I\), \(|B|=D\)). If moreover \(h\) is a graded basis of \(I\), then
\[\deg(p_jh_j)\le \max\{\deg(f),\ \deg(g_i)+2\deg(B):1\le i\le r\}.\]
As an optimization corollary (Cor. 1.1): for \(f^*=\min_S f\), if \((I:f-f^*)+(f-f^*)=(1)\) and \(h\) is a graded basis, then Lasserre/SoS exactness holds for
\[r\ge \tfrac12\max\{\deg(f),\ 2\deg(B)+\deg(g_1),\dots,2\deg(B)+\deg(g_r)\}.\]
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an explicit degree/order bound for exactness of Lasserre’s hierarchy over a finite complex variety assuming only that the homogenizations \(\bar g_1,\dots,\bar g_n\) form a regular sequence, without the “no solutions at infinity” condition used in Hua–Qu to get an \(H\)-basis/graded-basis property. This citing paper provides explicit degree bounds for exactness/finite convergence on finite semialgebraic sets, but the bounds require that the equality generators \(h\) form a graded basis of the zero-dimensional ideal \(I\) (equivalently, a strong degree-compatibility property akin to having an \(H\)-basis). Regular-sequence of homogenizations alone does not imply the graded-basis property, and the paper does not show how to derive a graded basis (or comparable truncation control) from the regular-sequence hypothesis when solutions at infinity exist. Thus it does not resolve the asked-for bound under only regular-sequence assumptions, but it gives closely related effective bounds in the stronger graded-basis setting and explicitly discusses Hua–Qu’s ‘no root at infinity’ hypothesis as a route to graded bases.
